guest_role: Founder and CEO, Character.AI (former 20-year Google Brain veteran)
assertiveness_why: Harry asks a genuinely hard, pointed challenge ('why is Character a standalone company and not a product of Facebook?') and probes tensions (generalist vs. specialized, open vs. closed, data vs. compute) but mostly lets Noam's answers stand without recontesting them.
question_style: Largely open-ended, letting Noam narrate at length, but Harry inserts direct challenge questions ('do you not worry we're losing touch with other humans?', the Facebook question) and plants a thesis (specialization drives richer conversation) for the guest to respond to; light on interruption.
tone: Enthusiastic and reverent toward a renowned technical figure, energetic fandom-tinged tone, some personal/reflective detours into parenthood and religion near the end.
depth: Goes deep on technical AI topics: compute vs. data as the constraint, dense vs. sparse computation and hardware-matched architectures, mixture-of-experts history, hallucination as feature vs. bug, proprietary vs. web-scraped training data, and open vs. closed model ecosystems.
harry_quotes: Do you not worry that we're losing touch with other humans?
Why is character a standalone company and not a product of Facebook?
What did you believe that you turned out to be wrong on?
learnings: Character.AI trained its production model for roughly $2 million in compute, with total training compute (size times duration) as the binding constraint rather than raw data volume.

The company deliberately launched a general-purpose product and let users discover unexpected use cases (like using a video-game character as an informal therapist) rather than picking a narrow vertical.
Noam sees hallucination as a feature to be embraced for entertainment/companionship use cases first, letting productivity applications mature later as the models improve.
Startups have an edge over incumbents like Facebook because large companies move slowly to protect existing products, though he expects room for many winners across both categories.

His early belief that sparse computation would be more efficient was wrong; the field's progress instead came from dense matrix multiplication matched to specialized hardware, which led him to design sparsity built from dense building blocks (mixture-of-experts).
notable: Part two of a special 'feature week' pairing two AI company founders; ends with an unusually personal detour into parenthood, religion, and responsibility.
